# Setup — getting face2face running

There are **two ways** to run this project. You do **not** need `docker-compose`.

| | Host (e.g. conda/anaconda) | Dev Container (recommended for C++) |
|---|---|---|
| Python proof-of-concept | works if you have the packages | works out of the box |
| Build the C++ pipeline (Eigen, OpenCV, HDF5, HighFive) | you must install all libs yourself | everything is preinstalled |
| Identical for the whole team | no | **yes** |

---

## 0. Get the data (everyone does this — it is NOT in git)

The BFM and Pandora are licensed, so `data/` is gitignored and shared by **no one**.
After cloning, each teammate obtains the data themselves and drops it in:

```
data/
├── bfm/model2017-1_bfm_nomouth.h5      # request: https://faces.dmi.unibas.ch/bfm/bfm2017/restricted/model2017-1_bfm_nomouth.h5 (is my personal link might not work)
└── pandora/base_1_ID01/                # request: https://www.dropbox.com/home/pandora/01/base_1_ID01 (Leo uploaded a subset -> he has to add you)
    ├── RGB/  DEPTH/  data.json  data.txt
```

Check it worked:
```bash
python3 python/check_bfm.py    # should print "vertices = 53149"
```

---

## Dev Container (tried to reacreate the env they gave us)

todo: add the dev container extension!

Bundles all C++ + Python dependencies in one Docker image, identical for everyone.
**No `docker-compose.yml` is involved** — the VS Code "Dev Containers" extension reads
`.devcontainer/devcontainer.json` + `Dockerfile` and starts the container for you.

**Prerequisites:** Docker, VS Code, and the **Dev Containers** extension
(`ms-vscode-remote.remote-containers`).

1. Open this folder in VS Code.
2. `Ctrl+Shift+P` → **"Dev Containers: Reopen in Container"**.
   (First build takes a few minutes; afterwards it is instant.)
3. Inside the container:
   ```bash
   # C++
   make run                        # build -> build/face_recon, then run it

   # Python proof-of-concept
   python3 python/main.py          # -> data/out/fit_result.png
   ```

> If the `Dockerfile` changes, run **"Dev Containers: Rebuild Container"** once.

### Without VS Code (plain Docker)

```bash
docker build -t facerecon -f .devcontainer/Dockerfile .
docker run --rm -it -v "$PWD":/workspace -w /workspace facerecon bash
# then run the same make / python3 commands inside
```



## What is in git vs not

- **In git (~112 KB):** source, headers, `Makefile`, `.devcontainer/`,
  `python/` (incl. `config.yaml`), docs.
- **Not in git:** `data/` (licensed BFM + Pandora, ~GBs), `build/`,
  `__pycache__/`, the Docker image itself. The libraries are installed *inside*
  the image from the `Dockerfile`; only the recipe is committed, never the libs.

---

## Troubleshooting

<!-- Section generated by Claude (Anthropic Claude Code) on 2026-06-12. -->

### Container build fails: `Something wicked happened resolving 'archive.ubuntu.com'`

Happens on **IPv6-mostly networks like TUM eduroam** (DNS64/NAT64). Docker
Desktop's VM then returns IPv6-only DNS answers, but build containers have no
IPv6 → `apt-get` inside the build cannot download anything (image *pulls* still
work, which makes it confusing). Verified on 2026-06-12 on eduroam.

**Fix 1 — easiest:** run the first build on a different network (home WiFi or
phone hotspot). Docker caches the layers, so afterwards "Reopen in Container"
works on any network.

**Fix 2 — works on eduroam:** route apt through a tiny HTTP proxy on your host
(the host's networking handles IPv6/DNS64 fine):

```bash
pip install proxy.py
python3 -m proxy --hostname 127.0.0.1 --port 3128 --log-level ERROR &

docker build -t facerecon \
  --build-arg http_proxy=http://host.docker.internal:3128 \
  --add-host host.docker.internal=host-gateway \
  -f .devcontainer/Dockerfile .

kill %1   # stop the proxy again
```

The proxy build-arg is **not** stored in the image or the layer cache key, so a
normal VS Code **"Reopen in Container"** afterwards hits the cached layers and
succeeds without any proxy.
